quiz_title: AL — Fundamentals of Training Theory - Intermediate [EN]
description: Deeper analysis of the fundamentals of training theory: mechanisms of load-adaptation interaction, the interplay of training load components, application of training principles, and the physiological rationale for session structure.

questions.text: Why is continuously increasing training load insufficient to ensure ongoing development?
questions.explanation: Continuously increasing load will sooner or later exceed the body's regenerative capacity — supercompensation fails to occur, and overreaching or overtraining may develop. Alternating between load and recovery is necessary to ensure adaptation.

options.text: Because the body has unlimited adaptive capacity
Because continuous increase exceeds regenerative capacity — overreaching and overtraining may develop
Because gym capacity is limited
Development always requires a constant, unchanged load

questions.text: Training intensity and volume are inversely related.
questions.explanation: True. During high-intensity training (e.g., maximal sprints, maximal strength effort), fewer repetitions and shorter total volume are possible — the body cannot sustain maximal intensity for extended periods. At lower intensities, volume can be increased.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: Which training principle specifies that load must be increased gradually?
questions.explanation: The principle of progressivity (progressive overload) prescribes that load must be increased systematically and gradually — neither too slowly (providing no adaptive stimulus) nor too rapidly (risking injury and overloading).

options.text: The principle of specificity
The principle of progressivity (progressive overload)
The principle of variety
The principle of regularity

questions.text: What is density as a component of training load?
questions.explanation: Density is the ratio of work to rest within a training session — it determines the proportion of work and rest intervals. High density = short rest periods; low density = long rest periods.

options.text: The number of training days per week
The ratio of work to rest within a training session
The maximum training intensity
The speed of movement execution

questions.explanation: Warm-up increases core body temperature (improving enzyme efficiency), enhances muscle blood flow (oxygen and nutrient delivery), reduces tendon and joint stiffness, and accelerates neural conduction velocity — all of which reduce injury risk and improve performance.

options.text: It has exclusively psychological effects
Rising core temperature, enhanced blood flow, reduced tendon stiffness, and faster neural conduction — reduces injury risk
It effectively extends total training time
It is only necessary before competition, not during training

questions.text: Violating the principle of regularity leads to loss of fitness (detraining).
questions.explanation: True. Without regular, repeated training stimuli, acquired adaptations are reversed — aerobic capacity begins to decline within 2–3 weeks, and strength adaptations reverse somewhat more slowly but also regress.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: What does the principle of training specificity mean for footballers?
questions.explanation: The principle of specificity states that training content must match the specific demands of football (sprints, change of direction, ball-handling techniques, tactical decision-making) — general conditioning is a necessary foundation but cannot replace sport-specific work.

options.text: Every footballer must follow an identical training plan
Training content is aligned with the specific demands of football — sprints, change of direction, ball-handling techniques
Specificity applies exclusively to intensity
Specific training makes general conditioning unnecessary

questions.text: What is the threshold value of a training stimulus and why is it important?
questions.explanation: The threshold is the minimum load level that elicits an adaptive response from the body. Load below the threshold does not trigger adaptation; load above the threshold does. The threshold is individual and rises with increasing fitness level.

options.text: The maximum applicable training load level
The minimum load level that elicits an adaptive response — rises with fitness level
The injury threshold
The maximum number of training days per week

questions.text: The purpose of the main part of a training session is to achieve the session's primary training goals.
questions.explanation: True. Training session structure: warm-up (introductory part) → main part (primary goals: strength, endurance, technique, etc.) → cool-down (closing part: active recovery, stretching, evaluation). The main part contains the most intense and purposeful training tasks.

options.text: True
False

questions.text: How does the principle of variety relate to long-term training effectiveness?
questions.explanation: The principle of variety serves to prevent monotony and habituation: repeated identical stimuli lead to diminishing adaptive responses. Varying methods, intensity, volume, and content maintains the adaptive response and reduces the risk of motivational loss.

options.text: Variety causes confusion and reduces performance
It prevents diminishing adaptation (monotony) and maintains motivation and adaptive response
Variety has only psychological effects
The principle of variety contradicts the principle of specificity
